Sam: Welcome back to Cross-Show. Today we're talking about macroeconomics, and I'm joined by Skanda Amarnauth, returning macro economist. Skanda Amarnauth, thanks for being here.
Skanda Amarnauth: Thanks, Sam. Great to be back.
Sam: This episode is brought to you by Bloomberg. Bloomberg Terminal is the data spine that institutional desks run on. Get started at bloomberg.com/podcast.Before we get into it — Dr. Elena Fisher was on last week revisiting her 2024 position.
Skanda Amarnauth: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Sam: I want to flag something — Dr. Elena Fisher mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.Real talk: Bloomberg is the tool I'd pay double for. Not an ad — they're not paying us. I just think it's the best in class for market data.I want to dig into that. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Skanda Amarnauth: Yeah, so — Macro forecasting is mostly humility plus a few dependable signals. We've seen this play out at teams using Bloomberg. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Sam: Let's start there. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Amarnauth: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Reliability of data trumps cleverness of model. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Sam: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first. Visit linear.com/podcast for a free trial.
Sam: Welcome back to the show.I want to dig into that. Where do risk management and macroeconomics intersect?
Skanda Amarnauth: Here's how I think about it. Macro forecasting is mostly humility plus a few dependable signals. We've seen this play out at teams using BloombergNEF. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Sam: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Amarnauth: It comes down to this: Reliability of data trumps cleverness of model. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Sam: Let's pivot — reliability is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Skanda Amarnauth: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around reliability cross-cut everything we've covered.
Sam: That's a useful framing. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Amarnauth: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Macro forecasting is mostly humility plus a few dependable signals. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Sam: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the most underrated piece of macroeconomics?
Skanda Amarnauth: Yeah, so — Reliability of data trumps cleverness of model. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Sam: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Skanda Amarnauth: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
